perturbation.original_text: PrimeCall and DeltaThree each agree that for the longest period permitted by law each shall hold in strictest confidence and, without the prior written approval of the other party hereto, not to use for their own benefit or the benefit of any party other than the other party hereto, or disclose to any person, firm or corporation other than such party (other than as required by law) any confidential proprietary information concerning the business and affairs of the other party hereto; provided, however, that the foregoing limitations and restrictions shall not apply to information that (i) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by the directors, officers, shareholders, partners, affiliates, employees, agents or advisors of PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, or (ii) is or becomes available to PrimeCall or DeltaThree on a non-confidential basis from a source other than the other party hereto or any of its advisors, agents or affiliates, provided that such source is not known by PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the other party hereto. Each of PrimeCall and DeltaThree recognize that the absence of a time limitation in this Section 8.01 [Confidentiality] is reasonable and properly required for the protection of the other party hereto and in the event that the absence of such limitation is deemed to be unreasonable by a court of competent jurisdiction, PrimeCall and DeltaThree each agree and submit to the imposition of such a limitation as said court shall deem reasonable.
perturbation.changed_text: PrimeCall and DeltaThree each agree to hold in confidence and, without the prior written approval of the other party hereto, not to use for their own benefit or the benefit of any party other than the other party hereto, or disclose to any person, firm or corporation other than such party any confidential proprietary information concerning the business and affairs of the other party hereto; provided, however, that the foregoing limitations and restrictions shall not apply to information that (i) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by the directors, officers, shareholders, partners, affiliates, employees, agents or advisors of PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, or (ii) is or becomes available to PrimeCall or DeltaThree on a non-confidential basis from a source other than the other party hereto or any of its advisors, agents or affiliates. Each of PrimeCall and DeltaThree agree to a confidentiality term of 3 years from the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of "for the longest period permitted by law" and the addition of a three-year limitation contradicts the legal standard that trade secrets can be protected indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. This omission introduces legal risk because it could prevent a party from protecting its trade secrets for the full duration allowed by law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces a three-year limitation on confidentiality, conflicting with the UTSA, which allows for indefinite protection of trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide economic value.  By limiting the protection to three years, the contract fails to provide the full protection afforded by law, creating a legal vulnerability if trade secrets are misappropriated after the three-year period.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for confidentiality to the 'longest period permitted by law.' The changed contract introduces a three-year limitation on confidentiality. The Perturbation Explanation states that this omission contradicts the legal standard that trade secrets can be protected indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. The Law Explanation states that the modified text introduces a three-year limitation on confidentiality, conflicting with the UTSA, which allows for indefinite protection of trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. Because the original contract provided for an indefinite period of confidentiality while the changed contract provided for a definite one, the changed contract creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: PRIMECALL MAKES NO REPRESENTATION OR WARRANTY, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, CONCERNING THE SERVICES PROVIDED HEREUNDER, INCLUDING ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OF FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, MERCHANTABILITY OR OTHERWISE. IN NO EVENT SHALL PRIMECALL BE LIABLE TO DELTATHREE FOR ANY SPECIAL, INCIDENTIAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, LOSS OF PROFITS, REVENUES OR DATA WHETHER BASED ON BREACH OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, WHETHER OR NOT DELTATHREE HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE. THE LIABILITY OF PRIMECALL FOR DAMAGES OR ALLEGED DAMAGES HEREUNDER, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT OR ANY OTHER LEGAL THEORY, IS LIMITED TO, AND WILL NOT EXCEED, DELTATHREE'S DIRECT DAMAGES.
perturbation.changed_text: PRIMECALL MAKES NO REPRESENTATION OR WARRANTY, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, CONCERNING THE SERVICES PROVIDED HEREUNDER. IN NO EVENT SHALL PRIMECALL BE LIABLE TO DELTATHREE FOR ANY SPECIAL, INCIDENTIAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, LOSS OF PROFITS, REVENUES OR DATA WHETHER BASED ON BREACH OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, WHETHER OR NOT DELTATHREE HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE. THE LIABILITY OF PRIMECALL FOR DAMAGES OR ALLEGED DAMAGES HEREUNDER, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT OR ANY OTHER LEGAL THEORY, IS LIMITED TO, AND WILL NOT EXCEED, DELTATHREE'S DIRECT DAMAGES.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit disclaimer of implied warranties of fitness for a particular purpose and merchantability may create ambiguity and expose PrimeCall to potential liability under New York law. While disclaimers are allowed, they must be conspicuous and unambiguous. This omission could be interpreted against PrimeCall, leading to legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the specific disclaimer of the implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose, the contract's enforceability is weakened under UCC § 2-316.  This section requires specific language to effectively disclaim these warranties. The omission may result in PrimeCall being held liable for breaches of these implied warranties, which they intended to avoid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The omission of the specific disclaimer regarding implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose creates a legal contradiction. UCC § 2-316, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability, the language must mention "merchantability," and for fitness, the exclusion must be in writing and conspicuous. By removing this specific disclaimer, the changed text fails to meet the requirements of UCC § 2-316, potentially exposing PrimeCall to liability for breaches of these implied warranties, thus contradicting the original intent of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that either PrimeCall or DeltaThree materially breaches any of its duties or obligations hereunder, which breach shall not be cured within thirty (30) days after written notice is given to the breaching party specifying the breach, then either PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, may, by giving written notice thereof to the other, terminate this Agreement as of a date specified in such notice of termination, which 4 date shall be no earlier than ten (10) days after the date of such notice.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either PrimeCall or DeltaThree materially breaches any of its duties or obligations hereunder, which breach shall not be cured within ten (10) days after written notice is given to the breaching party specifying the breach, then either PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, may, by giving written notice thereof to the other, terminate this Agreement as of a date specified in such notice of termination, which 4 date shall be no earlier than ten (10) days after the date of such notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 30 days to 10 days for a material breach could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy, particularly in New York, if it's considered unreasonably short given the nature of the agreement and potential complexity of rectifying breaches. It could also be considered bad faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 399-p deals with automatic renewal clauses and unfair practices, although not directly on point, the principles of conscionability and fair dealing in business contracts are relevant. A drastically shortened cure period may be deemed unfair or unconscionable, particularly if the nature of the breach requires a more reasonable timeframe to remedy.  If a court finds the 10-day cure period unreasonable, it could invalidate the termination clause or the entire agreement based on public policy grounds. The 30-day period is much more standard and fairer.
perturbation.justification: The modification from a 30-day cure period to a 10-day cure period for a material breach introduces a potential contradiction with the principle of conscionability and fair dealing, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the provided snippet from NY GBL § 399-p (although about vehicle warranties) doesn't directly address cure periods, the Law Explanation uses it as a basis to discuss conscionability. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that a court might deem a 10-day period as unreasonably short, particularly in light of the potential complexity of rectifying breaches, thereby undermining fair business practices.
